Psalm 92 begins with something that seems almost shockingly simple: it is good to praise the Lord. The psalmist does not apologize for worship or qualify it as something for the especially pious. Praise is good—as fundamental and self-evident as health or nourishment. Yet the psalm immediately deepens this goodness by locating praise in time: morning and night, the poles of every day. The worshiper does not offer sporadic emotional outbursts but a rhythm of remembrance. In the morning, celebrating God's loving devotion; in the night, His faithfulness. The instruments enter the sanctuary—the ten-stringed harp, the lyre—and suddenly we realize that this praise is not private sentiment but lived expression, sensory and full-bodied. The psalmist has been glad because of God's deeds, has seen the works of His hands with eyes that connect observation to worship. This is the framework: praise rooted in what God actually does, offered in every season, shaped by both intellect and instrument.

But then the psalmist turns to face something darker. There are senseless people, fools who fail to understand a terrible visual reality: the wicked flourish like grass, evildoers thrive and multiply. If you observe the world without this psalm's perspective, it appears that wickedness wins. The lush growth of the wicked is no metaphor—it is visible, undeniable, discouraging. This is the psalmist's confrontation. He does not deny the flourishing of evil; he denies that this flourishing means anything ultimate. The wicked will be destroyed forever. The Lord is exalted forever. These two statements collide. One is a promise about what appears now; the other is a declaration about what actually is. God's exaltation is not something that will happen—it is what already is, while the enemy's destruction, though not yet visible, is equally certain. This paradox is what separates the fool from the wise: the fool sees the wicked flourish and makes his conclusions there. The wise sees and then looks further, to God.

The psalmist moves from cosmic certainty to personal vindication, and this too is honest. His horn is exalted like a wild ox; he has been anointed with fine oil. He watches his enemies fall and hears their wailing. This is not pious detachment—the righteous person knows the satisfaction of vindication, the reality of opposition overcome. Yet this personal victory, striking as it is, becomes the image for something greater: the righteous themselves will flourish like a palm tree, like a cedar in Lebanon. Planted in the house of the Lord, they will bear fruit even in old age, remaining healthy and green. This is not the temporary vigor of grass but the deep, lasting strength of ancient trees. The wicked burst forth and fade; the righteous are planted and endure. And in that endurance—in that greenness that refuses to wither—they proclaim the character of God: He is upright, a Rock, without unrighteousness. The final proclamation does not come from special knowledge but from the lived experience of one who has been sustained, vindicated, and nourished by God's faithfulness across seasons.

What is demanded of you now, as you finish this psalm, is this: do you believe it? Not whether the words are beautiful, but whether the vision is true. Do you believe that God's exaltation is present now, not future? Do you believe that the apparent thriving of wickedness changes nothing about His ultimate victory? And do you believe that your life, if planted in His house, can bear fruit when you are old, can remain green while others wither? These are not questions for reflection alone. The psalmist has given you instruments. He has given you morning and night. He has given you permission to notice both the flourishing of evil and the faithfulness of God. Now praise.
